proposals.old_exact: [¶24] When reviewing the denial of a motion to suppress, this Court: "defers to the district court's findings of fact and resolves conflicts in testimony in favor of affirmance. Generally, a district court's decision to deny a motion to suppress will be affirmed if there is the sufficient competent evidence capable of supporting court's findings and if its decision is not contrary to the manifest weight of the evidence. Questions of law are fully reviewable on appeal, and whether a finding of fact meets a legal standard is a question of law." State v. Canfield, 2013 ND 236, ¶ 6, 840 N.W.2d 620 (cleaned up).
proposals.new_exact: [¶24] When reviewing the denial of a motion to suppress, this Court: "defers to the district court's findings of fact and resolves conflicts in testimony in favor of affirmance. Generally, a district court's decision to deny a motion to suppress will be affirmed if there is sufficient competent evidence capable of supporting the court's findings and if its decision is not contrary to the manifest weight of the evidence. Questions of law are fully reviewable on appeal, and whether a finding of fact meets a legal standard is a question of law." State v. Canfield, 2013 ND 236, ¶ 6, 840 N.W.2d 620 (cleaned up).
proposals.source: 2025ND65.pdf, lines 403-408 (block quote of State v. Canfield precedent)
proposals.note: Two ingestion errors in the quoted text from State v. Canfield: (1) will be affirmed if there is the sufficient should omit the before sufficient; (2) supporting court's findings should include the before court's. Both confirmed in authoritative PDF.

coverage_note: Full text comparison completed. Opinion is clean except for two related OCR errors within a single block quotation of precedent.

proposals.evidence: The PDF shows the justices' names listed on separate lines with consistent indentation (6 spaces). The DB incorrectly joined all justices' names into a single line in paragraph 23. This is a signature block formatting error where the ingestion process failed to preserve the line structure. The error is consistent with split_join type errors (wrongly fused words/lines).

coverage_note: Comprehensive review complete: compared database text against authoritative PDF source via pdftotext extraction. All substantive text verified as matching. Only structural formatting differences identified are two section marker splits (A and B), both confirmed as transcription-level errors. Caption formatting is layout-only (Appellant/Appellee positioning on same vs. separate lines)—a pdftotext rendering artifact, not a substantive error."

Complete transcription verified against PDF source. All 9 paragraphs of the opinion text checked. Word-by-word comparison (after removing page furniture) shows perfect match. Quote characters verified as proper curly quotes (U+201C/U+201D). No OCR errors, character substitutions, or transcription defects detected. Differences found are formatting only (indentation of section markers, PDF line breaks, page numbers)."

flags.location_quote: [¶10] Edwards argues the child's testimony was bolstered by inadmissible hearsay provided by a nurse practitioner and a detective. Terrilyn Braasch, the nurse practitioner who examined the child, testified: "Q: Okay. You referenced some terminology that the child used. Did she make any statements to you about the allegations? A: So when asking her in our review of systems which basically you just ask general questions, when I come to the part of her genital urinary system I asked her if it ever hurt when she urinated. And she said yes, after she was wiggled with." Edwards did not object. Detective Keith Miller later testified: "Q: And how did that report come to you? A: We received a 960 forwarded to us from Child Protective Service. In the 960 the foster parent who made the 960 referenced when she was changing Jane Doe's brother that Jane Doe had referenced her brother's penis, stating that [Edwards] had one and that he wiggled it by her. . . . Q: Did Jane Do make any disclosures...
flags.description: Ellipsis discrepancy in quoted testimony. DB contains '. . . .' (four spaced dots) after 'by her.' but authoritative PDF source shows '...' (three dots). This appears in unblocked quoted speech (Detective Miller's testimony) where three-dot ellipsis convention indicates omission/interruption. Distinct from block-quoted text in ¶7 which correctly shows four spaced dots. The count discrepancy (4 vs 3) combined with the context (quoted dialogue vs formal block quote) suggests potential transcription question rather than simple spacing artifact.

coverage_note: Complete verification of 2025 ND 42 (Interest of S.F.). All opinion paragraphs [¶1]-[¶4], case citations (2024 ND 43, 2024 ND 9, 2014 ND 28, 2010 ND 149), statutory references (N.D.C.C. § 27-20.3-20(1)(c)(2), N.D.R.Juv.P. 2, N.D.R.App.P. 35.1), party names (S.F., O.J.-G., F.F.), party count (450 of 660 nights), attorney names, and judicial panel verified against authoritative PDF source. No OCR errors, character substitutions, digit errors, or missing text detected. Differences from PDF are formatting only (line breaks, page furniture, caption alignment)."
